coredata.dc:title: Eosinophilic nitrogen-doped carbon dots derived from tribute chrysanthemum for label-free detection of Fe3+ ions and hydrazine
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of the Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2017 Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Lina
Fan, Hao
Huang, Lixin
Xia, Jianling
Huang, Jinrui
Li, Mei
Ding, Haiyang
Huang, Kun
Li, Shouhai
coredata.dc:description: A facile, green and economic one-step hydrothermal synthesis route using widely available tribute chrysanthemum as the new carbon and nitrogen precursors towards N-doped carbon nanodots (N-CDs) is proposed. The obtained N-CDs can be employed as acidophilic fluorescent sensor for label-free sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ in a strong acid environment with a detection limit as low as 0.001µM. In addition, the as-prepared N-CDs contain distinctive COOH groups and pyridyl nitrogen atoms on their surfaces. We further demonstrate that such wholly new N-CDs can serve as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for hydrazine. This environmentally friendly strategy offers a simple and efficient approach for preparing C-dots doped with heteroatoms from biomass in large-scale and lays a foundation for its further environmental applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chrysanthemum
Carbon nanodots
Fluorescent sensor
Heteroatoms
Acidophilic
Iron ions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence assay of Fe3+ and hydrazine

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A low-cost, green route to construct a novel sensing platform for Fe3+ and hydrazine detection based on N-doped C-dots by using tribute chrysanthemum through one-step treatment.
(a) TEM image of N-CDs. (b) Raman spectrum of N-CDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of N-CDs. (b, c) High-resolution XPS spectra of the C 1s and N 1s peaks of N-CDs, respectively. (d) FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of N-CDs. (b) The excitation and emission fluorescence spectra of N-CDs.
PL intensity of N-CDs dispersed in different pH solutions.
Quenching performance of the N-CDs solutions containing different metal ions ([Mn +]=50µM, F0 and F correspond to N-CDs PL intensities at 514nm in the absence and presence of ions, respectively).

The difference in PL intensity of N-CDs dispersion under various conditions ([Fe3 +]=50µM; [Cu2 +]=[Ca2 +]=[Al3 +]=[Cr3 +]=5mM, F0 and F correspond to N-CDs PL intensities at 514nm in the absence and presence of ions, respectively.).

(a) PL spectra of N-CDs in pH=4 aqueous solutions with different concentrations of Fe3+. (b) Stern–Volmer plot of Fe3+ obtained with N-CDs (F0 and F correspond to N-CDs PL intensities at 514nm in the absence and presence of ions, respectively).

(a) PL spectra of probe N-CDs with the addition of increasing concentration of hydrazine (0–100µM). (b) Stern–Volmer plot of hydrazine obtained with N-CDs (F0 and F correspond to N-CDs PL intensities at 514nm in the absence and presence of ions, respectively).

head.ce:title: Eosinophilic nitrogen-doped carbon dots derived from tribute chrysanthemum for label-free detection of Fe3+ ions and hydrazine

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Institute of Chemical Industry of Forestry Products, CAF; Jiangsu Province Biomass Energy and Materials Laboratory; National Engineering Laboratory for Biomass Chemical Utilization; Key and Lab. on Forest Chemical Engineering, SFA, Nanjing 210042, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Chemical Industry of Forestry Products, CAF; Jiangsu Province Biomass Energy and Materials Laboratory; National Engineering Laboratory for Biomass Chemical Utilization; Key and Lab. on Forest Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile, green and economic one-step hydrothermal synthesis route using widely available tribute chrysanthemum as the new carbon and nitrogen precursors towards N-doped carbon nanodots (N-CDs) is proposed. The obtained N-CDs can be employed as acidophilic fluorescent sensor for label-free sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ in a strong acid environment with a detection limit as low as 0.001µM. In addition, the as-prepared N-CDs contain distinctive COOH groups and pyridyl nitrogen atoms on their surfaces. We further demonstrate that such wholly new N-CDs can serve as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for hydrazine. This environmentally friendly strategy offers a simple and efficient approach for preparing C-dots doped with heteroatoms from biomass in large-scale and lays a foundation for its further environmental applications.

Image, graphical abstract
A low-cost, green route to construct a novel sensing platform for Fe3+ and hydrazine detection based on N-doped C-dots by using chrysanthemum through one-step treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: The ‘zero-dimensional’ fluorescent carbon nanodots (CDs) have attracted tremendous research interest due to their intriguing luminescent properties, high chemical inertness, excellent biocompatibility, good water solubility, and facile functionalization [1–4]. On the other hand, the production of CDs is always via facile synthetic routes with renewable and cheap raw materials not required for costly, intricate, or tedious preparation steps [5,6]. As a consequence of their outstanding optical and electronic properties, CDs become the most promising alternatives conventional organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots, and hold great promise in large-scale production and can be applicable in many areas such as photovoltaic devices, imaging agents and optoelectronic devices [7–11].

Recently, CDs containing heteroatoms have fuelled intensive research interest, as they exhibit unique tunable intrinsic properties, which are considered to complement carbon in material applications [12–15]. Particularly, considerable research efforts have been focused on preparing nitrogen-doped C-dots, as the introduction of nitrogen atoms appear to offer improved fluorescent quantum yield [16–20]. To incorporate N into the carbon matrix is thought to offer surface reaction activity, restrain non-radiative decay processes and facilitate electron transfer. Up to now, several strategies have been developed to incorporate nitrogen groups into the carbon framework, such as treatment of carbon materials with ammonia at high temperatures, strong acid carbonization of nitrogen containing compounds (NCCs), liquid phase polymerization of NCCs, and chemical vapor deposition [21–24]. Nevertheless, most of these developed methods are more or less unsatisfactory for practical applications such as low yields, high cost, harsh synthetic conditions, or complicated procedures. On the other hand, nearly all the florescent of reported C-dots are quenched or inactivated at low pH, and they can only exhibit a sensitive detection of metal ion in near-neutral aqueous solutions for metal ion sensing process, which restrict their applicability [25–30].

Tribute chrysanthemum, as a kind of natural resources of Huang Shan (Anhui Province, China), rich in carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen, is widely used as ingredients of Traditional Chinese Medicines. More importantly, the resourceful free amino acid in tribute chrysanthemum flowers may serve as source of N which could enable intrinsic, homogeneous doping of carbon framework without the extrinsic doping sources. Herein, we present a facile synthesis of N-doped C-dots (N-CDs) based on a one-step hydrothermal pyrolysis method starting from widely available chrysanthemum as the new carbon and nitrogen precursors (Scheme 1 ). The N-CDs emit strong blue luminescence at high acidophilic condition, with the fluorescence intensity very weak in a neutral and alkaline solution. As a result, the N-CDs can serve as a fluorescent probe for the detection of H+. What's more, the provided carbon nanodots can also be used as a novel florescent probe for highly sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ ions and hydrazine in aqueous media samples.

Tribute chrysanthemum was purchased from supermarket (Nanjing, China). All other reagents used in this paper were analytical grade and used as received. Inorganic salts NaNO3, KNO3, MnCl2, CaCl2, ZnCl2, NiCl2, CoCl2, Mg(OAc)2, CuCl2, CdCl2, AlCl3, and FeCl3 were dissolved in double distilled water (100mL) to obtain 50µM aqueous solutions. All samples were prepared at room temperature. Tap water samples were obtained from the Nanjing, Jiangsu province, China. Microencapsulated oil was purchased from Singao Co., Ltd., the microencapsulation sample was treated as according to Ng et al and then the upper clear solution was collected to evaluate the Fe3+ sensor for real sample analysis [31].

Fluorescence measurements were carried out on a Varian Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Varian, USA). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of the N-doped C-dots were measured by a Nicolet iS10 FT-IR spectrometer. UV–vis absorption spectra were carried out on a Shimadzu UV-2550PC spectrometer. The morphologies of the composites were observed by a transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Technai G2 20 S-Twin), Raman spectroscopy (LabRAM Aramis, laser excitation at 532nm), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ULVAC-PHI INC, PHI 5000 VersaProbe, Al Kα ). The binding energy of XPS spectrum was in reference to C 1s at 284.6eV.

In a typical synthesis of N-CDs, 1.0g of dry tribute chrysanthemum and 30mL distilled water were transferred into a 50mL Teflon equipped stainless steel, and then heated at 180°C for a period of 24h. After cooling down to ambient temperature naturally, the N-CDs dispersed in water were collected by removing the large dots though centrifugation at 12,000rpm for 10min, and then filtered through the syringe filter (0.22µm). Finally, a pale yellow acidic N-CDs solution was obtained with a pH value of approximately 4.0 for further characterization and use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence assay of Fe3+ and hydrazine
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of Fe3+ ions and hydrazine were performed at room temperature. Test solutions were prepared by placing 3.0mL of the stock solution into a cuvette, followed by the addition of a calculated amount of Fe3+ ions or hydrazine. The photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra were recorded after reaction for 15min at room temperature. The PL spectra of other metal ion solutions were also conducted to confirm the selectivity for Fe3+ ions in a similar way.

Tribute chrysanthemum is a well-known herb that has been widely used as a health food and medicine in the East Asian countries. It contains a wide variety of chlorogenic acid, flavonoids, phenolic acids, etc. [32]. In this study, the preparation of N-CDs was completed by employing tribute chrysanthemum as the raw material via one step hydrothermal treat. The obtained N-CDs were dispersed well in water. TEM images are used to determine the morphology and the size distribution of N-CDs. Fig. 1 a shows N-CDs display fairly monodisperse nanoparticles of near spherical morphology. The diameters of the N-CDs are mainly distributed in the range of 10–19nm with an average diameter of 14.36nm by DLS (Fig. S1). Raman spectrum was measured to distinguish sp2-hybridized carbon from sp3-hybridized carbon atoms. Two bands at ca. 1330 and 1596cm−1 were detected as shown in Fig. 1b, which could be attributed to the D band (disordered sp3 carbon) and G band (E2g vibration mode of sp2 domains of graphene), indicating the graphitized nature of N-CDs [33].

XPS and FT-IR spectra analysis were recorded to identify the functional groups of the N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2 a, three dominant peaks at ca. 285, 401 and 532eV are ascribed to C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, respectively. The elemental analysis shows that the composition of the N-CDs were C (91.64at.%), N (1.98at.%), O (6.35at.%) and S (0.03at.%). The detailed C 1s spectrum (Fig. 2b) exhibits three different peaks corresponding to the presence of 284.5, 285.4, and 289.1eV, respectively, attributable to the CC/CC, CO/CN/C-S, and CO(N) groups. The detailed N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) exhibits four different peaks corresponding to the presence of pyridinic-N (ca. 398.4eV), pyrrolic-N (ca. 399.9eV), graphitic-N (ca. 401.2eV) and oxidized-N (ca. 403.2eV), which show the successful incorporation of intrinsic N into the sp2 carbon matrix [34]. Fig. 2d exhibits the characteristic absorption band of aromatic NH at ca. 760 and 3320cm−1, CN heterocycles at ca. 1669cm−1, OCO at ca. 1582cm−1 and of OH at ca. 3100 and 1385cm−1, which indicates the obtained C-dots contains carboxyl group, hydroxyl group and CN units [35–36]. Therefore, the XPS and FT-IR results confirm that the new N-CDs are successfully obtained.

The UV–vis absorption and PL spectra were carried out to investigate the optical properties of the N-CDs. As seen from Fig. 3 a, the characteristic adsorption peak around 248nm and a shoulder peak at 274nm represent a typical absorption of an aromatic π system, which could be ascribed to the π→π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains and the n→π* transition of CO groups, respectively. The absorption shoulder near 318nm can be ascribed to the n→π* transition, as the contribution of the energy trap stemmed from the heteroatom-passivated surface state. The result indicates that nitrogen is a strong absorber of UV radiation. This presence of surface states can cause strong emission features in N-doped CDs. As seen in Fig. 3b, the N-CDs possess the strongest excitation and emission peaks at ca. 453nm and 514nm, respectively. Moreover, the wavelength of the emission peak of N-CDs is almost the same as the excitation peak, which suggests that little non-emitting absorption has been generated in N-CDs. Using quinine sulfate as a standard, the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) (excited at 362nm) of the N-CDs is calculated to be 17.3%. In comparison, the QY of undoped CDs is determined to be less than 10% according to other published literatures, suggesting that nitrogen doping can remarkably increase the photoluminescence properties of the CDs, which may be ascribed to the introduction of more passivated surface defects on the CDs surface [5,35].

To investigate the optical responses of the N-CDs to various pH values ranging from 1 to 12, the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs with different pH values were observed with luminescence spectrometer and showed in Fig. 4 . The fluorescence activity of the N-CDs was found to be pH-dependent, which surprisingly indicated an increase in the fluorescence intensity as the pH decreases, and the maximum intensity is approached at pH=2. The evolution of the fluorescence intensity can be separated into two ranges. The N-CDs show good fluorescence activities in acidic conditions, while relatively low in neutral and alkaline solutions. In the pH range of 4–12, the fluorescence intensity is significantly decreased as the pH value increases. This behavior may be attributed to the different deprotonation degrees of the N-doped CDs at various pH values [37]. The deprotonation degree gradually increases when the pH value decreases, resulting in a higher net surface charge which endows the N-CDs with dispersibility and hydrophilic properties in water, and consequently enhancing the fluorescence intensity. In particular, the increasing rate of fluorescence intensity decelerates with decreasing pH values in the range of pH=2–4, possibly because the protonation of N-CDs tend to be saturated in an acid environment. Thus, considering conventional waste water conditions in an acid environment, these N-CDs have potential applications in a strong acid environment.

In recent years, many reported CDs sensors are inactivated or quenched at low pH (pH<6) and can only make a sensitive response for metal ion in near-neutral or alkaline conditions, which restricts their application in a real world context of environmental pollution [38]. Because the as-obtained N-CDs possess excellent fluorescence properties in acid solutions and the abundant oxygen functional groups on the surface of N-CDs can interact with metal ions to form coordinated complexes, so, the N-CDs can potentially be used as fluorescent sensor for metal ions in acid environment. Therefore, the feasibility of N-CDs as the sensor for metal ion detection in acid solutions is investigated. We carried out PL measurements in the presence of 11 types of metal ions such as Cu2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Cr3+ and Fe3+ under the same concentration and the results were shown in Fig. 5 . It is seen that N-CDs with Fe3+ system has the strongest fluorescence quenching efficiency, whereas negligible interference on the fluorescence behaviors of N-CDs with other metal ions, indicating that the obtained N-CDs possess excellent sensing selectivity toward Fe3+. The high selectivity of the prepared sensor for Fe3+ is probably attributed to that Fe3+ has higher and faster chelating process with N-CDs than other metal ions by the formation of Fe3+ N-CDs via coordination reaction of Fe3+ with affinity groups COOH or OH on the surface of N-CDs. Furthermore, we also performed Fe3+ detection by mixing Fe3+ with the presence of 100-fold Cu2+, Ca2+, Al3+, or Cr3+ as competing metal ions to investigate the effectiveness of N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 6 , Fe3+ still exhibits a similar fluorescence quenching behavior without being significantly affected by the interfering metal ions, indicating the high selectivity of N-CDs towards Fe3+ in acid environment.

We investigated the sensitivity and feasibility of using such N-CDs for quantitative detection of Fe3+ by titration of the N-CDs with Fe3+ (pH 4.0). It is seen that the solution of N-CDs in the absence of metal ions exhibited a strong PL peak at 514nm (Fig. S2, curve a). In contrast, the presence of Fe3+ leads to an obviousdecrease in the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs, indicating that Fe3+ can effectively quench the fluorescence of N-CDs (Fig. S2, curve b).

For the sensitivity study, various concentrations of Fe3+ in the range of 0–500µM were investigated. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the PL intensity of the N-CDs is sensitive to Fe3+ concentration and decreases gradually as the Fe3+ concentration increases. From Stern–Volmer plot analysis, we found that the ratios of F0/F have a good linear correlation (R2 =0.98) over the concentration range of 0–100µM (Fig. 7b), which confirmed previous results that Fe3+ interacts with N-CDs by chelating with affinity groups on the surface of N-CDs resulting in fluorescence quenching. The detection limit is about 0.001M (at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3), which is much lower than the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency allowed maximum tolerance (5.4µM) in drinking water [39]. As shown in Table S1, the results of N-CDs sensor for detecting Fe3+ are compared with that of other reported carbon dots-based sensors in near-neutral aqueous solutions, which demonstrates superior sensitivity and low detection limit of the prepared N-CDs sensor.

Furthermore, the fluorescence responses for Fe3+ in a real world context are evaluated. The fluorescence experiments for real water sample and microencapsulated oil sample analysis were then repeated using tap water samples obtained from Nanjing, Jiangsu province and microencapsulated oil purchased from Singao Co.,Ltd. As shown in Figs. S3 and S4, the PL intensity of N-CDs decreases with increased concentration of Fe3+ from 0 to 10µM. Despite the interference from numerous minerals and organics existing in real samples, the proposed sensing platform is still effective for distinguishing between contaminated and non-contaminated water, providing the practical Fe3+ detection in real samples.

It has been reported that the molecular structure containing COOH or pyridyl nitrogen atoms could have been applied for sensing hydrazine by forming composite which facilitate charge transfer and restrain the recombination of excitons resulting in a significant fluorescence enhancement [40–42]. Herein, for the N-CDs, the pyridyl nitrogen atoms and COOH groups on their surfaces can also react with hydrazine. Based on this mechanism, we examine the practicality of using the N-CDs for the determination of hydrazine. Hydrazine has been widely used in various industry areas, such as corrosion inhibitor, pharmaceuticals, pesticides, emulsifiers, dyes and so on. However, hydrazine is highly toxic to humans causing adverse systemic health effects and damage to the kidneys, liver, and lungs. Recently, fluorescence probes have become a powerful tool for detecting hydrazine for their sensitivity and selectivity. However, these probes are mostly obtained by difficult and complex synthesis steps, and time-consuming sample pretreatment procedures. Therefore, the design of convenient and inexpensive approaches by utilizing full intrinsic components of biomass materials for the detection of hydrazine is in ever-increasing demand.

Under the conditions mentioned above, we investigated the capability of this sensing platform for quantitative detection of hydrazine. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the FL intensity of N-CDs is sensitive to hydrazine concentration and increases gradually as the continuous addition of hydrazine. Moreover, the FL intensity enhancement is linearly related to the hydrazine concentration in the range of 0–100µM with a correlation coefficient of R2 =0.97 (Fig. 8b).To evaluate the selectivity of N-CDs toward hydrazine, we examined the FL intensity changes in the presence of representative ions under the same conditions, including cations (represented by Cu2+, Cd2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, and Al3+) and primary amines (represented by glutamine, thiourea, ethylenediamine, and hydroxylamine), and no obvious changes in the emission spectra of N-CDs were observed (Fig. S5). These results imply that the developed sensing system exhibits excellent selectivity of hydrazine. The outstanding selectivity and specificity can be probably attributed to that N-CDs can respond to hydrazine by the pyridyl nitrogen atoms and OCO groups on the N-CDs surface than other analytes.

In conclusion, novel N-CDs were fabricated by dehydration and carbonization of chrysanthemum without extrinsic doping of N from exogenous dopants. The resulting N-CDs exhibit a strong and stable fluorescence emission with a high quantum yield (up to 17%) in acidic conditions. Remarkably, the obtained N-CDs exhibit multi-purpose applications for high selective detection of Fe3+ and hydrazine. This efficient strategy is facile and low cost, which has potential applications for developing novel environmental applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Jiangsu Province Biomass Energy and Materials Laboratory, China (grant number: JSBEM-S-201603).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.jtice.2017.06.011.
